title: Emergency Guide: Cat Choking Sounds and Pawing at Mouth
meta_desc: Is your cat choking and pawing at their mouth? Learn the emergency steps to take, what causes this behavior, and when to see a vet immediately.
content_html:
What is it?

When a cat makes choking sounds while pawing at their mouth, it is a sign of acute distress. This behavior often indicates that something is physically stuck in the oral cavity, stuck in the back of the throat, or caught on the tongue. Common culprits include string, sewing needles, small toy parts, or bone fragments. In some cases, it may also be caused by a severe dental injury or an allergic reaction causing the throat to swell.

Is it an emergency?

Yes. This is a High Urgency situation. If your cat is actively struggling to breathe, has a blue or purple tint to their gums, or is losing consciousness, you must transport them to the nearest emergency veterinary clinic immediately. Do not attempt to reach into the cat's mouth if they are panicking, as you may be bitten or accidentally push the object deeper into the airway.

Why a Photo or Video Helps Triage

If you can safely record a quick 5-10 second video of the behavior, it can be life-saving for your veterinarian. Many conditions, such as feline asthma, hairballs, or reverse sneezing, can mimic choking. A video allows the vet to differentiate between a respiratory issue and a physical obstruction. Similarly, a clear photo of any visible object in the mouth helps the surgical team prepare for immediate removal before you even walk through the door.

faqs.question: How can I tell the difference between choking and a hairball?
faqs.answer: Retching for a hairball usually involves rhythmic abdominal contractions and ends with the cat coughing up fur. Choking is more frantic, involves pawing at the face, and the cat may produce high-pitched wheezing or no sound at all.

faqs.question: Should I try to pull a string out of my cat's mouth?
faqs.answer: No. If you see a string, do not pull it. It may be anchored further down in the digestive tract, and pulling it can cause life-threatening lacerations to the esophagus or intestines.

faqs.question: What if my cat stops choking before we get to the vet?
faqs.answer: You should still have them evaluated. Small fragments of an object may remain, or the object may have caused internal trauma to the throat that requires anti-inflammatory or antibiotic treatment.
